Process documented for initiating re-assessment upon environmental change
Evidence of feedback loop from monitoring into re-assessment
evidence_anti_signals: No schedule or triggers for re-assessment
HUDERIA treated as one-time compliance activity
No mechanism to trigger review after system updates or incidents
controls.remediation_template: If iterative review is not established, the organization must define and document triggers and a schedule for revisiting the HUDERIA, ensuring it is updated after any major system change or environmental shift.
controls.framework_name_en: Common Guideposts to Promote Interoperability in AI Risk Management
controls.framework_name_ar: مرتكزات مشتركة لتعزيز قابلية التشغيل البيني في إدارة مخاطر الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Concept of Operations and Context Exploration Process
controls.control_text_full: Concept of Operations and Context Exploration Process, to identify values and conduct a feasibility analysis. Includes understanding the ethical environment for system deployment and defining operational expectations.
controls.intent_summary: Before design begins, teams must define the intended operational context of the AI system, including its social, legal, and ethical environment, to ensure alignment with real-world use conditions and ethical boundaries.
evidence_signals: Documented operational context including deployment settings, user groups, and environments
Ethical environment analysis referencing relevant norms or regulations
Operational expectations signed off by product and ethics leads
Feasibility analysis including ethical constraints as criteria
Mapping of context to potential ethical risks
evidence_anti_signals: No documented operational context
Context described only in technical terms without ethical or social dimensions
Operational expectations undefined or assumed
controls.remediation_template: If context exploration is missing, the team must produce a formal operational context document that describes deployment settings, user groups, and ethical constraints, and obtain sign-off from ethics and legal stakeholders.
controls.framework_name_en: Common Guideposts to Promote Interoperability in AI Risk Management
controls.framework_name_ar: مرتكزات مشتركة لتعزيز قابلية التشغيل البيني في إدارة مخاطر الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ethical Values Elicitation and Prioritisation Process
controls.control_text_full: Ethical Values Elicitation and Prioritisation Process, to consider ethical questions and priorities and to engage stakeholders. Includes defining and ranking ethical values to be implemented in system design and obtaining approval from management and other stakeholders.
controls.intent_summary: Teams must explicitly identify, discuss, and prioritize ethical values (e.g., fairness, transparency, accountability) relevant to the system, engage stakeholders in this process, and secure formal approval before proceeding to design.
evidence_signals: List of prioritized ethical values with rationale for ranking
Stakeholder input records from elicitation sessions
Management approval record for the prioritized values
Mapping of values to specific system features or design choices
Evidence of diverse stakeholder participation (e.g., end users, civil society)
evidence_anti_signals: Values listed without prioritization or justification
No record of stakeholder input or consultation
Values defined solely by engineers without oversight
controls.remediation_template: If ethical values are not elicited and prioritized, the team must conduct structured workshops with diverse stakeholders to define and rank values, document the process, and obtain formal management approval before design begins.
controls.framework_name_en: Common Guideposts to Promote Interoperability in AI Risk Management
controls.framework_name_ar: مرتكزات مشتركة لتعزيز قابلية التشغيل البيني في إدارة مخاطر الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ethical Requirements Definition Process
controls.control_text_full: Ethical Requirements Definition Process, to develop risk mitigation strategies based on ethical value requirements.
controls.intent_summary: Ethical values must be translated into specific, testable design requirements that directly mitigate identified risks, ensuring values are not abstract but embedded into system specifications.
evidence_signals: List of ethical requirements derived from prioritized values
Each requirement linked to a specific risk and value
Requirements written in measurable or verifiable language (e.g., 'system must detect bias with >90% accuracy')
Traceability matrix linking values → requirements → design components
Review by ethics or compliance team on requirement validity
evidence_anti_signals: Ethical requirements are vague (e.g., 'be fair') without measurable criteria
No traceability from values to technical requirements
Requirements defined after design is complete
controls.remediation_template: If ethical requirements are not defined, the team must convert each prioritized ethical value into at least one measurable, testable system requirement and document the mapping to associated risks and design elements.
controls.framework_name_en: Common Guideposts to Promote Interoperability in AI Risk Management
controls.framework_name_ar: مرتكزات مشتركة لتعزيز قابلية التشغيل البيني في إدارة مخاطر الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ethical Risk-Based Design Process
controls.control_text_full: Ethical Risk-Based Design Process, to translate ethical value requirements and risk management policies into implementable engineering targets.
controls.intent_summary: Engineers must convert ethical requirements into concrete technical specifications and engineering targets, ensuring that ethical design is not conceptual but implemented in code, architecture, or interfaces.
evidence_signals: Engineering specifications tied to ethical requirements (e.g., 'model must log decision rationales')
Architecture diagrams showing ethical controls (e.g., audit trails, bias detection modules)
Code reviews or testing protocols for ethical compliance
Performance metrics for ethical outcomes (e.g., fairness thresholds)
Evidence of collaboration between engineers and ethics team during design
evidence_anti_signals: Ethical requirements exist but are not implemented in design
No engineering specs or code changes linked to ethical goals
Design decisions made without ethics team input
controls.remediation_template: If ethical requirements are not implemented in design, the engineering team must revise system architecture and code to include measurable ethical controls, with documentation showing traceability from requirements to implementation.
controls.framework_name_en: Common Guideposts to Promote Interoperability in AI Risk Management
controls.framework_name_ar: مرتكزات مشتركة لتعزيز قابلية التشغيل البيني في إدارة مخاطر الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Transparency Management Process
controls.control_text_full: Transparency Management Process that extends through both stages to ensure appropriate communication with stakeholders in the design process.
controls.intent_summary: Throughout design and development, teams must maintain ongoing, accessible communication with stakeholders about how ethical considerations are being addressed, ensuring trust and accountability.
evidence_signals: Public or stakeholder-facing documentation on ethical design choices
Regular updates shared with users or community representatives
Transparency reports or dashboards showing ethical performance
Mechanisms for stakeholders to ask questions or raise concerns
Evidence of transparency activities documented in project logs
evidence_anti_signals: No communication with stakeholders beyond internal teams
Ethical design decisions kept secret or only shared with management
No public or accessible transparency artifacts
controls.remediation_template: If transparency is not managed, the team must establish a public or stakeholder-accessible channel to communicate ethical design decisions, provide regular updates, and document all transparency activities.
controls.framework_name_en: Common Guideposts to Promote Interoperability in AI Risk Management
controls.framework_name_ar: مرتكزات مشتركة لتعزيز قابلية التشغيل البيني في إدارة مخاطر الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Risk Assessment and Reduction Process
controls.control_text_full: ISO/IEC Guide 51 sets out how risks can be understood, in terms of severity and likelihood, and describes an iterative process to identify, estimate and reduce risks to 'tolerable levels'.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must undergo a formal risk assessment that quantifies potential harms by severity and likelihood, and implements controls to reduce risks to levels deemed acceptable or 'tolerable' by safety standards.
evidence_signals: Risk matrix with severity and likelihood ratings for each identified harm
Documented risk reduction measures with effectiveness estimates
Evidence of iterative reassessment after mitigation
Definition of 'tolerable risk' level based on recognized safety norms
Risk assessment covering intended use and foreseeable misuse
evidence_anti_signals: No quantification of severity or likelihood
Risk reduction measures not linked to specific risks
Tolerable risk level undefined or arbitrarily set
controls.remediation_template: If risk assessment is not performed using severity and likelihood, the team must adopt a formal risk matrix, quantify each risk, define tolerable thresholds, and document iterative reduction efforts.
controls.framework_name_en: Common Guideposts to Promote Interoperability in AI Risk Management
controls.framework_name_ar: مرتكزات مشتركة لتعزيز قابلية التشغيل البيني في إدارة مخاطر الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Monitoring Risk Reduction Efforts and Documentation
controls.control_text_full: It also includes requirements for monitoring risk reduction efforts and documenting risk information.
controls.intent_summary: Organizations must continuously monitor whether risk mitigation measures remain effective and maintain comprehensive, accessible records of all risk-related decisions and actions taken.
evidence_signals: Monitoring logs or dashboards tracking risk mitigation effectiveness
Documented risk register updated regularly with audit trails
Retention policy for risk documentation aligned with legal requirements
Evidence of periodic audits of risk controls
Risk documentation accessible to auditors or regulators
evidence_anti_signals: No monitoring of mitigation effectiveness over time
Risk documentation incomplete, outdated, or inaccessible
No retention policy or records destroyed after deployment
controls.remediation_template: If monitoring and documentation are lacking, the organization must implement a risk register with regular updates, define monitoring metrics, and ensure all risk records are retained and accessible for review.
controls.framework_name_en: Common Guideposts to Promote Interoperability in AI Risk Management
controls.framework_name_ar: مرتكزات مشتركة لتعزيز قابلية التشغيل البيني في إدارة مخاطر الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Implement the Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.control_text_full: Each Member State is required to submit the Recommendation to its competent authorities within a period of one year from the close of the session of the General Conference at which it was adopted, i.e., before 24 November 2022. As requested by the General Conference, Member States will then be consulted in order to establish a report on the measures taken to implement the Recommendation. This report will be presented at the 43rd session of the General Conference.
controls.intent_summary: Member States must formally adopt the UNESCO AI Ethics Recommendation into their national governance structures and report back on implementation steps. This ensures global accountability and alignment with international ethical standards for AI.
evidence_signals: National legislation or executive order citing the UNESCO Recommendation
Official government report submitted to UNESCO by 24 November 2022
Publicly accessible AI ethics policy document referencing UNESCO’s framework
Minutes from national consultations with civil society on AI ethics
Submission confirmation from UNESCO’s Secretariat
evidence_anti_signals: No national policy or legal instrument referencing the Recommendation
Absence of any public reporting on implementation status
Statements denying applicability of international AI ethics norms
controls.remediation_template: The organization must demonstrate that it has submitted the UNESCO AI Ethics Recommendation to its national regulatory bodies and established a documented plan for implementation, with evidence of reporting to UNESCO.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Respect, protect, and promote human rights and fundamental freedoms
controls.control_text_full: The Recommendation presented here, as a standard-setting instrument developed through a global approach, based on international law, focusing on human dignity and human rights, as well as gender equality, social and economic justice and development, physical and mental well-being, diversity, interconnectedness, inclusiveness, and environmental and ecosystem protection can guide AI technologies in a responsible direction.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be designed and deployed in full alignment with international human rights law, ensuring that they uphold dignity, equality, and freedoms without discrimination or harm.
evidence_signals: Human rights impact assessments conducted for AI systems
Design documentation showing alignment with ICCPR, CEDAW, CRC, and other treaties
Audit trails demonstrating mitigation of discriminatory outcomes
Publicly available statements committing to human rights compliance
Training materials for developers on human rights-based AI design
evidence_anti_signals: Absence of human rights impact assessments
Use of biased training data without mitigation
Claims that human rights are 'not applicable' to AI systems
controls.remediation_template: AI developers and deployers must explicitly map their systems to relevant international human rights instruments and document how they prevent violations of dignity, equality, and freedoms.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ensure transparency, explainability, and interpretability of AI systems
controls.control_text_full: The need for transparency and understandability of the workings of algorithms and the data with which they have been trained.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be designed so that their decision-making processes and data sources can be understood and explained by users, regulators, and affected individuals to enable accountability and trust.
evidence_signals: Model cards or technical documentation disclosing training data sources
Explainability tools (e.g., LIME, SHAP) integrated into deployed systems
Publicly accessible documentation on algorithmic logic and limitations
User-facing explanations for automated decisions
Third-party audits verifying transparency claims
evidence_anti_signals: Black-box systems with no documentation of internal logic
Claims that 'explainability is not technically feasible' without justification
Use of proprietary algorithms to avoid disclosure
controls.remediation_template: Organizations must provide clear, accessible documentation explaining how their AI systems make decisions, including data provenance and model logic, to enable meaningful oversight.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Prevent discrimination, bias, and exclusion
controls.control_text_full: AI technologies can be of great service to humanity and all countries can benefit from them, but also raise fundamental ethical concerns, for instance regarding the biases they can embed and exacerbate, potentially resulting in discrimination, inequality, digital divides, exclusion and a threat to cultural, social and biological diversity and social or economic divides.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be actively audited and designed to avoid reinforcing or amplifying existing biases related to race, gender, disability, socioeconomic status, or geography, ensuring equitable outcomes for all.
evidence_signals: Bias audits using diverse datasets across demographic groups
Documentation of fairness metrics (e.g., disparate impact analysis)
Inclusive design teams with representation from marginalized communities
Publicly reported results of bias testing
Remediation plans for identified discriminatory outcomes
evidence_anti_signals: Use of non-representative training data without correction
Lack of demographic testing in model validation
Claims that 'the data is neutral' despite known historical biases
controls.remediation_template: Organizations must conduct and publish regular bias assessments across protected attributes and implement corrective measures to ensure AI systems do not perpetuate or worsen social inequalities.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Promote environmental sustainability and protect ecosystems
controls.control_text_full: AI technologies have the potential to be beneficial to the environment and ecosystems, and in order for those benefits to be realized, potential harms to and negative impacts on the environment and ecosystems should not be ignored but instead addressed.
controls.intent_summary: AI development and deployment must consider environmental costs, such as energy consumption and e-waste, and actively contribute to ecological protection rather than harm.
evidence_signals: Carbon footprint assessments for AI training and inference
Use of energy-efficient hardware or renewable-powered data centers
Documentation of environmental impact mitigation strategies
Public commitments to green AI standards
Monitoring of water and resource usage in AI infrastructure
evidence_anti_signals: No reporting on energy use or emissions from AI systems
Use of high-energy models without efficiency optimization
Claims that environmental impact is 'outside the scope' of AI ethics
controls.remediation_template: Organizations must measure and publicly report the environmental impact of their AI systems and adopt measures to minimize energy use, emissions, and resource depletion.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ensure equitable access and benefit-sharing, especially for LMICs
controls.control_text_full: Recognizing that AI technologies can deepen existing divides and inequalities in the world, within and between countries, and that justice, trust and fairness must be upheld so that no country and no one should be left behind, either by having fair access to AI technologies and enjoying their benefits or in the protection against their negative implications... Emphasizing that specific attention must be paid to LMICs, including but not limited to LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS, as they have their own capacity but have been underrepresented in the AI ethics debate...
controls.intent_summary: AI systems and their benefits must be made accessible to low- and middle-income countries, respecting local knowledge and ensuring that these nations are not excluded from participation, innovation, or protection.
evidence_signals: Partnerships with institutions in LMICs for AI development
Open-access datasets or models tailored for low-resource settings
Localization of AI tools in regional languages and cultural contexts
Capacity-building programs for AI governance in LMICs
Public commitments to technology transfer and equitable licensing
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems only available in high-income country languages or infrastructures
Exclusion of LMIC stakeholders from AI ethics consultations
Patents or proprietary systems blocking access in developing regions
controls.remediation_template: Organizations must demonstrate active efforts to ensure their AI technologies are accessible, affordable, and culturally appropriate for low- and middle-income countries, including through collaboration and capacity support.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Uphold cultural diversity and respect local values and knowledge
controls.control_text_full: Further recognizing that AI technologies have the potential to be beneficial to the environment and ecosystems, and in order for those benefits to be realized, potential harms to and negative impacts on the environment and ecosystems should not be ignored but instead addressed... requiring the recognition, protection and promotion of endogenous cultures, values and knowledge in order to develop sustainable digital economies...
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must respect and integrate local cultural norms, indigenous knowledge, and value systems rather than imposing homogenized or Western-centric models.
evidence_signals: Consultation with indigenous or local communities during AI design
Inclusion of culturally relevant datasets and ontologies
Documentation of cultural impact assessments
Use of local languages and context-aware interfaces
Recognition of traditional knowledge in AI training or deployment
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems trained solely on Western datasets without local adaptation
Disregard for local cultural norms in user interfaces or decision logic
Claims that 'global standards' override local values
controls.remediation_template: Organizations must engage with local communities to understand and integrate cultural context into AI design, ensuring systems respect and reflect diverse worldviews and knowledge systems.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Support media and information literacy to combat misinformation
controls.control_text_full: Recognizing that the development of AI technologies necessitates a commensurate increase in data, media and information literacy as well as access to independent, pluralistic, trusted sources of information, including as part of efforts to mitigate risks of misinformation, disinformation and hate speech, and harm caused through the misuse of personal data.
controls.intent_summary: AI developers and deployers must support public understanding of AI and digital information to reduce harm from misinformation, disinformation, and manipulation enabled by AI systems.
evidence_signals: Public education campaigns on AI literacy and digital rights
Partnerships with media organizations to promote trusted information
In-app prompts or tools helping users identify AI-generated content
Funding or support for independent fact-checking initiatives
Documentation of literacy initiatives integrated into product design
evidence_anti_signals: No efforts to educate users about AI-generated content
Platforms promoting viral misinformation without labeling
Claims that 'users should figure it out themselves'
controls.remediation_template: Organizations must invest in public education and user tools that enhance media and information literacy to counter AI-enabled misinformation and protect democratic discourse.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Foster ethical innovation and avoid stifling progress
controls.control_text_full: Noting that addressing risks and ethical concerns should not hamper innovation and development but rather provide new opportunities and stimulate ethically-conducted research and innovation that anchor AI technologies in human rights and fundamental freedoms, values and principles, and moral and ethical reflection.
controls.intent_summary: Ethical safeguards should not be used as barriers to innovation, but rather as frameworks that guide responsible development, ensuring innovation aligns with human dignity and societal well-being.
evidence_signals: Ethics review boards integrated into R&D pipelines
Documentation of ethical design choices that enabled innovation
Publicly shared ethical guidelines for AI development teams
Innovation awards for ethically designed AI solutions
Case studies showing how ethics improved product trust or adoption
evidence_anti_signals: Ethics teams sidelined or treated as compliance blockers
Projects abandoned due to fear of ethics review without justification
Claims that 'ethics slows us down' without proposing alternatives
controls.remediation_template: Organizations must integrate ethical review as a catalyst for innovation, not a bottleneck, and demonstrate how ethical considerations have led to better, more trusted AI solutions.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Apply the Recommendation on a voluntary basis through legislative or other measures
controls.control_text_full: Recommends that Member States apply on a voluntary basis the provisions of this Recommendation by taking appropriate steps, including whatever legislative or other measures may be required, in conformity with the constitutional practice and governing structures of each State, to give effect within their jurisdictions to the principles and norms of the Recommendation in conformity with international law, including international human rights law;
controls.intent_summary: Member States must implement the ethical principles of the Recommendation through domestic laws or policies, tailored to their constitutional frameworks, while ensuring alignment with international human rights law. This requires active governance action, not passive acknowledgment.
evidence_signals: National AI ethics policy document referencing UNESCO Recommendation
Legislative text amending data protection or algorithmic accountability laws to incorporate UNESCO principles
Governmental press release announcing adoption of UNESCO-aligned AI governance framework
Public register of AI systems reviewed for human rights compliance
Ministry of Justice training materials for judges on UNESCO AI ethics norms
evidence_anti_signals: Absence of any national AI policy or strategy
AI procurement guidelines that ignore human rights or non-discrimination requirements
Public statements dismissing UNESCO as 'non-binding' without proposing alternatives
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government should develop and enact a national AI governance framework that explicitly incorporates the UNESCO Recommendation’s principles and aligns with international human rights obligations.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Engage all stakeholders in implementation
controls.control_text_full: Also recommends that Member States engage all stakeholders, including business enterprises, to ensure that they play their respective roles in the implementation of this Recommendation; and bring the Recommendation to the attention of the authorities, bodies, research and academic organizations, institutions and organizations in public, private and civil society sectors involved in AI technologies, so that the development and use of AI technologies are guided by both sound scientific research as well as ethical analysis and evaluation.
controls.intent_summary: Governments must actively involve all actors in the AI ecosystem—businesses, researchers, civil society, and public institutions—to ensure ethical oversight throughout AI development and deployment, not just regulatory compliance.
evidence_signals: Stakeholder consultation records with industry, academia, and NGOs on AI ethics
Publicly accessible multi-stakeholder AI ethics advisory board minutes
University ethics review protocols citing UNESCO Recommendation
Corporate AI ethics guidelines developed in collaboration with civil society
Government-funded grants requiring stakeholder engagement as a condition
evidence_anti_signals: AI policy developed solely by technocrats without public or civil society input
No mention of private sector or academic roles in implementation plans
Ethics review processes limited to internal corporate teams only
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the government or organization should establish a formal multi-stakeholder engagement process that includes industry, academia, civil society, and marginalized communities to co-develop and review AI ethics practices.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Apply a holistic, multicultural, and evolving ethical framework to AI systems
controls.control_text_full: This Recommendation approaches AI ethics as a systematic normative reflection, based on a holistic, comprehensive, multicultural and evolving framework of interdependent values, principles and actions that can guide societies in dealing responsibly with the known and unknown impacts of AI technologies on human beings, societies and the environment and ecosystems, and offers them a basis to accept or reject AI technologies. It considers ethics as a dynamic basis for the normative evaluation and guidance of AI technologies, referring to human dignity, well-being and the prevention of harm as a compass and as rooted in the ethics of science and technology.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be evaluated using an ethical framework that is inclusive of diverse cultural values, adaptable over time, and centered on human dignity, well-being, and harm prevention—not just technical performance or efficiency.
evidence_signals: AI system impact assessments that include cultural context and human dignity analysis
Ethics review board charter explicitly citing multicultural and evolving principles
Training materials for developers on non-Western ethical perspectives in AI
Public documentation showing how local values shaped AI design choices
Regular updates to AI ethics guidelines reflecting new societal feedback
evidence_anti_signals: AI ethics guidelines based solely on Western liberal norms
Impact assessments focused only on accuracy or speed, ignoring cultural or social harm
No mechanism to update ethical standards as societal values evolve
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the AI system’s ethical design and evaluation process must be redesigned to incorporate diverse cultural perspectives and be formally updated in response to societal feedback, with human dignity and harm prevention as core metrics.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Address ethical issues across the entire AI system life cycle
controls.control_text_full: Ethical questions regarding AI systems pertain to all stages of the AI system life cycle, understood here to range from research, design and development to deployment and use, including maintenance, operation, trade, financing, monitoring and evaluation, validation, end-of-use, disassembly and termination.
controls.intent_summary: Ethical oversight must extend beyond just development or deployment—it must cover every phase of an AI system’s existence, from initial research through to decommissioning and disposal, ensuring accountability throughout its lifetime.
evidence_signals: AI project documentation with ethics checkpoints at each life cycle stage
Contractual clauses requiring ethical review during financing and trade phases
End-of-life protocols for AI systems including data deletion and hardware recycling
Audit trails showing ethical evaluations at validation and monitoring stages
Procurement specs requiring vendors to provide life cycle ethics reports
evidence_anti_signals: Ethics reviews limited to prototype or testing phase only
No documented process for ethical review during maintenance or decommissioning
Vendor contracts omitting obligations for ethical end-of-use practices
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the AI project must integrate mandatory ethical reviews at every stage of the system’s life cycle—from research to decommissioning—and document these reviews in a publicly accessible audit trail.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Recognize AI actors across all roles in the life cycle
controls.control_text_full: AI actors can be defined as any actor involved in at least one stage of the AI system life cycle, and can refer both to natural and legal persons, such as researchers, programmers, engineers, data scientists, end-users, business enterprises, universities and public and private entities, among others.
controls.intent_summary: All individuals and organizations contributing to any phase of an AI system’s life cycle—whether designing, using, funding, or maintaining it—are ethically responsible and must be held accountable for ethical outcomes.
evidence_signals: AI governance policy listing all actor types with assigned responsibilities
Contracts assigning ethical accountability to end-users or financiers
Training programs for data scientists and end-users on ethical responsibilities
Publicly disclosed roles of each actor in a deployed AI system
Liability clauses in procurement agreements covering all AI actors
evidence_anti_signals: Ethical responsibility assigned only to developers or vendors
No identification of end-users or financiers as AI actors in policy
Contracts that exempt non-developers from ethical obligations
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the organization must formally define and assign ethical responsibilities to every type of actor involved in the AI system’s life cycle—including end-users, financiers, and operators—and document these roles in governance policies.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Address AI’s impact on UNESCO’s core domains: education, science, culture, and communication
controls.control_text_full: This Recommendation pays specific attention to the broader ethical implications of AI systems in relation to the central domains of UNESCO: education, science, culture, and communication and information...
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be ethically evaluated for their specific impacts on education, scientific integrity, cultural diversity, and information access—ensuring they support, rather than undermine, UNESCO’s foundational missions in these areas.
evidence_signals: AI curriculum guidelines for schools incorporating critical AI literacy
Research ethics protocols requiring cultural impact assessments in AI-driven science
Cultural heritage digitization projects with community consent and representation
Media platforms with transparent AI moderation policies aligned with freedom of expression
Public reports on AI’s impact on linguistic diversity and local media ecosystems
evidence_anti_signals: AI tools deployed in education without critical thinking components
Cultural content algorithms favoring globalized content over local languages
News recommendation systems amplifying disinformation without ethical review
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, AI systems in education, science, culture, or information must undergo targeted ethical impact assessments aligned with UNESCO’s mandate in these domains, with public reporting on outcomes.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Mitigate algorithmic bias and reinforce discrimination
controls.control_text_full: Furthermore, new ethical challenges are created by the potential of AI algorithms to reproduce and reinforce existing biases, and thus to exacerbate already existing forms of discrimination, prejudice and stereotyping.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be actively designed and audited to prevent the replication or amplification of societal biases, particularly those related to race, gender, class, or geography, to avoid deepening systemic discrimination.
evidence_signals: Bias audits conducted using diverse demographic datasets
Publicly available fairness metrics for AI models (e.g., disparate impact scores)
Training data documentation showing efforts to correct underrepresentation
Redress mechanisms for individuals harmed by biased AI decisions
Procurement criteria requiring bias mitigation as a non-negotiable requirement
evidence_anti_signals: Training data lacking representation from marginalized groups
No testing for differential performance across demographic groups
Claims of 'neutral' algorithms without empirical validation
controls.remediation_template: If this control is not met, the AI system must undergo a comprehensive bias audit using representative data, implement mitigation strategies, and establish a transparent process for remediation of discriminatory outcomes.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Respect, protection and promotion of human rights and fundamental freedoms and human dignity
controls.control_text_full: The inviolable and inherent dignity of every human constitutes the foundation for the universal, indivisible, inalienable, interdependent and interrelated system of human rights and fundamental freedoms. Therefore, respect, protection and promotion of human dignity and rights as established by international law, including international human rights law, is essential throughout the life cycle of AI systems. Human dignity relates to the recognition of the intrinsic and equal worth of each individual human being, regardless of race, colour, descent, gender, age, language, religion, political opinion, national origin, ethnic origin, social origin, economic or social condition of birth, or disability and any other grounds. No human being or human community should be harmed or subordinated, whether physically, economically, socially, politically, culturally or mentally during any phase of the life cycle of AI systems. Throughout the life cycle of AI systems, the quality of life of human beings should be enhanced, while the definition of “quality of life” should be left open to individuals or groups, as long as there is no violation or abuse of human rights and fundamental freedoms, or the dignity of humans in terms of this definition. Persons may interact with AI systems throughout their life cycle and receive assistance from them, such as care for vulnerable people or people in vulnerable situations, including but not limited to children, older persons, persons with disabilities or the ill. Within such interactions, persons should never be objectified, nor should their dignity be otherwise undermined, or human rights and fundamental freedoms violated or abused.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must not harm, subdue, or objectify any person at any stage of their life cycle, and must uphold human dignity and rights as defined by international law. Designers and operators must ensure AI enhances human well-being without violating fundamental freedoms or dignity, especially for vulnerable populations.
evidence_signals: Documentation of human rights impact assessments for AI systems
Design guidelines prohibiting objectification of users in AI interactions
Audit logs showing mitigation of bias against protected groups
Training materials for developers on international human rights law
Policies explicitly prohibiting AI use that undermines dignity (e.g., surveillance of marginalized groups)
evidence_anti_signals: Absence of human rights impact assessments
Use of AI that automates discrimination without redress mechanisms
Marketing materials portraying AI as replacing human care without safeguards
Lack of consultation with vulnerable communities during system design
controls.remediation_template: If human dignity or rights are not being upheld in AI systems, implement a mandatory human rights impact assessment at each stage of the life cycle and establish an ethics review board with representation from affected communities.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Environment and ecosystem flourishing
controls.control_text_full: Environmental and ecosystem flourishing should be recognized, protected and promoted through the life cycle of AI systems. Furthermore, environment and ecosystems are the existential necessity for humanity and other living beings to be able to enjoy the benefits of advances in AI. All actors involved in the life cycle of AI systems must comply with applicable international law and domestic legislation, standards and practices, such as precaution, designed for environmental and ecosystem protection and restoration, and sustainable development. They should reduce the environmental impact of AI systems, including but not limited to its carbon footprint, to ensure the minimization of climate change and environmental risk factors, and prevent the unsustainable exploitation, use and transformation of natural resources contributing to the deterioration of the environment and the degradation of ecosystems.
controls.intent_summary: AI developers and users must minimize environmental harm throughout the AI life cycle, including energy use and resource extraction, and comply with environmental laws to protect ecosystems and combat climate change.
evidence_signals: Published carbon footprint metrics for AI training and inference
Use of renewable energy sources in data centers supporting AI
Environmental sustainability clauses in procurement contracts for AI services
Life cycle assessments (LCA) of AI systems including hardware and data usage
Adoption of energy-efficient model architectures or pruning techniques
evidence_anti_signals: No reporting on energy consumption or emissions from AI operations
Use of high-power models without justification or efficiency optimization
Procurement of AI systems from providers with known high environmental impact
Absence of environmental risk assessments in project plans
controls.remediation_template: If environmental impacts of AI systems are unaddressed, require all AI projects to conduct and publish a life cycle environmental assessment and adopt energy-efficient practices aligned with recognized sustainability standards.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ensuring diversity and inclusiveness
controls.control_text_full: Respect, protection and promotion of diversity and inclusiveness should be ensured throughout the life cycle of AI systems, consistent with international law, including human rights law. This may be done by promoting active participation of all individuals or groups regardless of race, colour, descent, gender, age, language, religion, political opinion, national origin, ethnic origin, social origin, economic or social condition of birth, or disability and any other grounds. The scope of lifestyle choices, beliefs, opinions, expressions or personal experiences, including the optional use of AI systems and the co-design of these architectures should not be restricted during any phase of the life cycle of AI systems. Furthermore, efforts, including international cooperation, should be made to overcome, and never take advantage of, the lack of necessary technological infrastructure, education and skills, as well as legal frameworks, particularly in LMICs, LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS, affecting communities.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be designed and deployed to include and empower all groups, especially marginalized and under-resourced communities, and must not exacerbate existing inequalities or exclude people due to lack of infrastructure or skills.
evidence_signals: Diverse datasets representing global populations including LMICs
Co-design processes involving communities from low-resource regions
Publicly available accessibility features for users with disabilities
Language support for non-dominant languages in AI interfaces
Partnerships with institutions in LMICs/LDCs for capacity building
evidence_anti_signals: Training data dominated by high-income country demographics
AI systems only available in major languages or requiring high-end hardware
Absence of community input in AI development cycles
Deployment of AI in regions without local digital infrastructure without support
controls.remediation_template: If inclusiveness is lacking, mandate inclusive design practices, including participatory development with underrepresented groups and equitable access initiatives targeting low-resource regions.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Living in peaceful, just and interconnected societies
controls.control_text_full: AI actors should play a participative and enabling role to ensure peaceful and just societies, which is based on an interconnected future for the benefit of all, consistent with human rights and fundamental freedoms. The value of living in peaceful and just societies points to the potential of AI systems to contribute throughout their life cycle to the interconnectedness of all living creatures with each other and with the natural environment. The notion of humans being interconnected is based on the knowledge that every human belongs to a greater whole, which thrives when all its constituent parts are enabled to thrive. Living in peaceful, just and interconnected societies requires an organic, immediate, uncalculated bond of solidarity.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must foster social cohesion, justice, and interconnectedness rather than division or exploitation, and should be designed to support peaceful, equitable, and solidarity-based societies.
evidence_signals: AI applications designed to bridge social divides (e.g., cross-cultural communication tools)
Ethical review criteria prioritizing social harmony and conflict prevention
Policies prohibiting AI use in surveillance or repression of dissent
Projects promoting civic participation through AI-enabled public engagement
Transparency mechanisms enabling public oversight of AI in governance
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems used to manipulate public opinion or spread disinformation
Deployment of AI in authoritarian contexts to suppress dissent
Lack of mechanisms for public accountability in AI-driven decision-making
Absence of social impact assessments focused on community cohesion
controls.remediation_template: If AI systems undermine social cohesion, require ethical impact assessments to evaluate effects on societal trust and justice, and prohibit uses that enable repression, manipulation, or social fragmentation.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Provide a universal framework of values, principles and actions to guide States in AI legislation and policy
controls.control_text_full: to provide a universal framework of values, principles and actions to guide States in the formulation of their legislation, policies or other instruments regarding AI, consistent with international law;
controls.intent_summary: Governments must develop AI laws and policies grounded in the UNESCO ethical framework, ensuring alignment with international human rights and sustainability obligations.
evidence_signals: National AI strategies citing UNESCO Recommendation as foundational
Legislative drafts incorporating UNESCO principles verbatim
Public consultations referencing UNESCO values in policy development
Ministries publishing compliance reports against UNESCO guidelines
Training programs for lawmakers on UNESCO AI ethics framework
evidence_anti_signals: National AI policies that ignore human rights or environmental principles
Absence of reference to UNESCO in official AI governance documents
Legislation that permits AI uses explicitly prohibited by UNESCO
No mechanism to align domestic AI policy with international norms
controls.remediation_template: If national AI policies lack alignment with UNESCO, require governments to revise legislation to explicitly incorporate the Recommendation’s values and principles, with public consultation and legal review.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Guide all AI actors to embed ethics throughout the AI system life cycle
controls.control_text_full: to guide the actions of individuals, groups, communities, institutions and private sector companies to ensure the embedding of ethics in all stages of the AI system life cycle;
controls.intent_summary: All entities involved in AI development or deployment — from researchers to corporations — must integrate ethical considerations into every phase of the AI life cycle, from design to decommissioning.
evidence_signals: Ethics review checkpoints in AI project workflows
Ethics training mandatory for all AI team members
Documentation of ethical risk mitigation at each life cycle stage
Publicly available AI ethics charters or codes of conduct
Audit trails showing ethical decision points in model development
evidence_anti_signals: No ethics training provided to AI teams
Ethics reviewed only at final stage or not at all
Absence of documented ethical considerations in technical reports
Ethics treated as optional or marketing add-on rather than core requirement
controls.remediation_template: If ethics are not embedded in the AI life cycle, mandate structured ethics checkpoints at each phase and require documented ethical impact assessments as part of project deliverables.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Promote equitable access to AI developments and benefit-sharing with LMICs
controls.control_text_full: to promote equitable access to developments and knowledge in the field of AI and the sharing of benefits, with particular attention to the needs and contributions of LMICs, including LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS.
controls.intent_summary: AI advancements and their benefits must be shared equitably, with special focus on supporting low-resource countries through technology transfer, capacity building, and inclusive participation.
evidence_signals: Open-source AI models or datasets shared with LMIC institutions
Funding programs for AI capacity building in developing nations
Joint research initiatives co-led by LMIC and high-income researchers
Policies requiring benefit-sharing agreements in AI partnerships
Training programs in LMICs on AI development and governance
evidence_anti_signals: AI technologies exclusively licensed or controlled by high-income entities
No engagement with LMICs in AI research or policy design
Data extraction from LMICs without local benefit or consent
Absence of equity clauses in international AI collaborations
controls.remediation_template: If AI benefits are not equitably shared, require all international AI projects to include concrete plans for capacity building, open access, and local benefit-sharing with LMICs, LDCs, LLDCs, and SIDS.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: No restriction on lifestyle choices, beliefs, expressions or personal experiences in AI life cycle
controls.control_text_full: The scope of lifestyle choices, beliefs, opinions, expressions or personal experiences, including the optional use of AI systems and the co-design of these architectures should not be restricted during any phase of the life cycle of AI systems.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must not restrict individuals' personal choices, beliefs, or expressions at any stage of their life cycle. This ensures autonomy and freedom in how people interact with or shape AI systems.
evidence_signals: AI system documentation explicitly states that user lifestyle choices are not restricted by design
User consent forms include opt-in options for co-design participation without coercion
Ethics review board reports confirm no restrictions on expression in training data or interfaces
Public-facing AI policies affirm freedom of expression as a design principle
User feedback mechanisms allow reporting of perceived restrictions on personal expression
evidence_anti_signals: AI system enforces mandatory behavioral patterns or content filters based on normative assumptions
Terms of service prohibit users from modifying or challenging AI outputs based on personal beliefs
Training data excludes diverse expressions or censors minority viewpoints
controls.remediation_template: If AI systems restrict personal choices or expressions, revise the design and policy to explicitly permit user autonomy and co-design without coercion.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Overcome technological and infrastructural gaps in LMICs, LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS
controls.control_text_full: Furthermore, efforts, including international cooperation, should be made to overcome, and never take advantage of, the lack of necessary technological infrastructure, education and skills, as well as legal frameworks, particularly in LMICs, LDCs, LLDCs and SIDS, affecting communities.
controls.intent_summary: AI actors must actively work to close technological and capacity gaps in vulnerable regions, and must never exploit their lack of infrastructure or legal frameworks. International solidarity is required.
evidence_signals: AI development projects include partnerships with local institutions in LMICs
Funding proposals explicitly allocate resources for capacity building in underserved regions
Public reports document investments in local digital literacy and infrastructure
AI procurement policies prioritize vendors with equitable global access commitments
Collaborative research papers co-authored by stakeholders from SIDS or LDCs
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems are deployed in low-resource regions without local adaptation or training
Data collection occurs in vulnerable communities without local capacity building
AI vendor contracts exclude obligations to support infrastructure development
controls.remediation_template: If AI initiatives neglect capacity building in vulnerable regions, revise procurement and deployment plans to include mandatory infrastructure, education, and legal support components.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: AI actors must promote peaceful and just interconnected societies
controls.control_text_full: AI actors should play a participative and enabling role to ensure peaceful and just societies, which is based on an interconnected future for the benefit of all, consistent with human rights and fundamental freedoms. The value of living in peaceful and just societies points to the potential of AI systems to contribute throughout their life cycle to the interconnectedness of all living creatures with each other and with the natural environment.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be designed and used to foster social cohesion, peace, justice, and ecological interconnectedness, avoiding actions that divide communities or harm the environment.
evidence_signals: AI system impact assessments include metrics on social cohesion and environmental impact
Project charters explicitly state goals of promoting peace and interconnectedness
Stakeholder engagement plans include community representatives from diverse backgrounds
Ethics reviews evaluate AI’s contribution to societal harmony and ecological balance
Public communications frame AI as a tool for collective well-being, not competition
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems are marketed as tools for surveillance or social control
Training data reinforces divisive narratives or environmental exploitation
No stakeholder engagement with marginalized or ecological groups
controls.remediation_template: If AI systems contribute to division or environmental harm, revise objectives and design to explicitly promote peace, justice, and ecological interconnectedness.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: AI must not segregate, objectify, or undermine freedom, safety, or coexistence
controls.control_text_full: This value demands that peace, inclusiveness and justice, equity and interconnectedness should be promoted throughout the life cycle of AI systems, in so far as the processes of the life cycle of AI systems should not segregate, objectify or undermine freedom and autonomous decision-making as well as the safety of human beings and communities, divide and turn individuals and groups against each other, or threaten the coexistence between humans, other living beings and the natural environment.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must avoid any design or deployment that segregates, objectifies, or endangers individuals, communities, or ecosystems. They must uphold freedom, safety, and ecological coexistence.
evidence_signals: AI system design documents include bias and harm mitigation strategies for segregation
Impact assessments evaluate effects on community cohesion and ecological balance
User interfaces avoid dehumanizing language or algorithmic categorization
Ethics audits confirm no use of AI to divide social groups
Environmental impact statements address AI’s footprint on biodiversity
evidence_anti_signals: AI systems categorize users into hierarchical or exclusionary groups
Training data reinforces stereotypes that divide communities
No evaluation of AI’s impact on non-human life or ecosystems
controls.remediation_template: If AI systems risk segregation, objectification, or ecological harm, redesign to embed principles of inclusion, safety, and coexistence throughout the life cycle.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Proportionality and Do No Harm in AI deployment
controls.control_text_full: It should be recognized that AI technologies do not necessarily, per se, ensure human and environmental and ecosystem flourishing. Furthermore, none of the processes related to the AI system life cycle shall exceed what is necessary to achieve legitimate aims or objectives and should be appropriate to the context. In the event of possible occurrence of any harm to human beings, human rights and fundamental freedoms, communities and society at large or the environment and ecosystems, the implementation of procedures for risk assessment and the adoption of measures in order to preclude the occurrence of such harm should be ensured.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be proportionate to their intended purpose, avoid unnecessary harm, and include proactive risk assessment and mitigation measures to prevent harm to people, rights, or ecosystems.
evidence_signals: Risk assessment reports document potential harms and mitigation plans
System documentation justifies AI use as the least intrusive option available
Ethics review boards require proportionality analysis before deployment
Impact assessments include environmental and societal harm thresholds
Audit trails show mitigation actions taken after harm identification
evidence_anti_signals: AI deployed without any documented risk assessment
System uses excessive data or processing power beyond what is needed
No mechanism to halt deployment when harm is detected
controls.remediation_template: If AI systems lack proportionality or harm prevention measures, implement mandatory risk assessments and ensure all deployments are the least intrusive means to achieve legitimate aims.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Justification of AI method choice must meet three criteria
controls.control_text_full: The choice to use AI systems and which AI method to use should be justified in the following ways: (a) the AI method chosen should be appropriate and proportional to achieve a given legitimate aim; (b) the AI method chosen should not infringe upon the foundational values captured in this document, in particular, its use must not violate or abuse human rights; and (c) the AI method should be appropriate to the context and should be based on rigorous scientific foundations. In scenarios where decisions are understood to have an impact that is irreversible or difficult to reverse or may involve life and death decisions, final human determination should apply. In particular, AI systems should not be used for social scoring or mass surveillance purposes.
controls.intent_summary: Any AI method must be justified as appropriate, rights-respecting, scientifically sound, and context-sensitive. Human decision-making must remain final in high-stakes scenarios, and AI must never be used for social scoring or mass surveillance.
evidence_signals: Technical documentation includes justification for method selection against alternatives
Human rights impact assessments are conducted and signed off before deployment
Peer-reviewed research underpins the AI model’s methodology
Explicit policy prohibits use of AI for social scoring or mass surveillance
Final decision authority is retained by humans in medical, judicial, or safety-critical contexts
evidence_anti_signals: AI method selected without comparison to non-AI alternatives
No evidence of scientific validation or peer review
AI used to assign social credit scores or monitor populations at scale
controls.remediation_template: If AI methods lack justification or are used for prohibited purposes like social scoring, halt deployment and require documented justification meeting all three criteria before reconsideration.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Ensure safety and security throughout AI life cycle
controls.control_text_full: Unwanted harms (safety risks), as well as vulnerabilities to attack (security risks) should be avoided and should be addressed, prevented and eliminated throughout the life cycle of AI systems to ensure human, environmental and ecosystem safety and security. Safe and secure AI will be enabled by the development of sustainable, privacy-protective data access frameworks that foster better training and validation of AI models utilizing quality data.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be designed and operated to prevent all forms of safety and security risks—both accidental and malicious—throughout their life cycle, using secure, privacy-protective data practices.
evidence_signals: Security penetration tests are conducted at each life cycle stage
Data access frameworks comply with privacy-by-design standards
Incident response plans exist for AI-related safety breaches
Model validation includes adversarial testing for vulnerabilities
Audit logs track data handling and system access for security accountability
evidence_anti_signals: No security testing performed on AI models
Training data is accessed via unencrypted or unauthenticated channels
No documented response plan for AI system compromise
controls.remediation_template: If AI systems lack safety or security controls, implement lifecycle-wide risk mitigation, secure data frameworks, and regular penetration testing.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Promote fairness, non-discrimination, and inclusive access to AI
controls.control_text_full: AI actors should promote social justice and safeguard fairness and non-discrimination of any kind in compliance with international law. This implies an inclusive approach to ensuring that the benefits of AI technologies are available and accessible to all, taking into consideration the specific needs of different age groups, cultural systems, different language groups, persons with disabilities, girls and women, and disadvantaged, marginalized and vulnerable people or people in vulnerable situations. Member States should work to promote inclusive access for all, including local communities, to AI systems with locally relevant content and services, and with respect for multilingualism and cultural diversity. Member States should work to tackle digital divides and ensure inclusive access to and participation in the development of AI. At the national level, Member States should promote equity between rural and urban areas, and among all persons regardless of race, colour, descent, gender, age, language, religion, political opinion, national origin, ethnic origin, social origin, economic or social condition of birth, or disability and any other grounds, in terms of access to and participation in the AI system life cycle. At the international level, the most technologically advanced countries have a responsibility of solidarity with the least advanced to ensure that the benefits of AI technologies are shared such that access to and participation in the AI system life cycle for the latter contributes to a fairer world order with regard to information, communication, culture, education, research and socio-economic and political stability.
controls.intent_summary: AI must be designed and deployed equitably, ensuring access and participation for all groups—especially marginalized ones—across national and international boundaries, with special attention to digital divides and inclusion.
evidence_signals: AI training datasets include representation from diverse demographics and languages
Public AI services are available in multiple languages and accessible to persons with disabilities
Inclusion plans document outreach to rural, indigenous, or low-income communities
International partnerships include capacity transfer to LMICs
Equity audits assess access disparities across gender, age, disability, and geography
evidence_anti_signals: AI services only available in dominant languages or urban centers
Training data excludes marginalized groups or reinforces stereotypes
No budget or plan for inclusive access or digital inclusion
controls.remediation_template: If AI systems exclude or disadvantage marginalized groups, revise design and deployment to ensure equitable access, representation, and participation across all demographics and geographies.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

related_icaire_terms.term_en: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)

controls.control_title: Minimize and avoid reinforcing bias in AI systems
controls.control_text_full: AI actors should make all reasonable efforts to minimize and avoid reinforcing or perpetuating discriminatory or biased applications and outcomes throughout the life cycle of the AI system to ensure fairness of such systems. Effective remedy should be available against discrimination and biased algorithmic determination.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must actively prevent bias and discrimination at every stage, and provide accessible, effective remedies when biased outcomes occur.
evidence_signals: Bias audits are conducted at data, model, and deployment stages
Remediation pathways are documented and accessible to affected users
Feedback loops allow users to report discriminatory outcomes
Model cards disclose known biases and mitigation steps
Diverse teams review training data and outputs for discriminatory patterns
evidence_anti_signals: No bias testing performed
Users have no way to challenge algorithmic decisions
Discriminatory outcomes are dismissed as 'technical limitations'
controls.remediation_template: If AI systems produce biased outcomes without remedy mechanisms, implement continuous bias testing and establish accessible, transparent channels for redress.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Address digital and knowledge divides in AI life cycle
controls.control_text_full: Furthermore, digital and knowledge divides within and between countries need to be addressed throughout an AI system life cycle, including in terms of access and quality of access to technology and data, in accordance with relevant national, regional and international legal frameworks, as well as in terms of connectivity, knowledge and skills and meaningful participation of the affected communities, such that every person is treated equitably.
controls.intent_summary: AI development must actively reduce digital and knowledge gaps by ensuring equitable access to technology, data, skills, and participation opportunities for all communities.
evidence_signals: AI projects include digital literacy training for local users
Data collection respects local connectivity constraints and uses low-bandwidth methods
Community members are co-designers or advisors in AI development
Access to AI tools is provided offline or via low-cost devices
Training materials are available in local languages and formats
evidence_anti_signals: AI tools require high-speed internet or expensive hardware with no alternatives
Local communities are consulted only after deployment
Training data is inaccessible due to language or technical barriers
controls.remediation_template: If AI systems exacerbate digital or knowledge divides, redesign access models to include low-resource, community-led, and multilingual solutions.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Assess AI’s impact on sustainability and SDGs
controls.control_text_full: The development of sustainable societies relies on the achievement of a complex set of objectives on a continuum of human, social, cultural, economic and environmental dimensions. The advent of AI technologies can either benefit sustainability objectives or hinder their realization, depending on how they are applied across countries with varying levels of development. The continuous assessment of the human, social, cultural, economic and environmental impact of AI technologies should therefore be carried out with full cognizance of the implications of AI technologies for sustainability as a set of constantly evolving goals across a range of dimensions, such as currently identified in the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of the United Nations.
controls.intent_summary: AI systems must be continuously evaluated for their impact on sustainability goals, including environmental, social, and economic dimensions aligned with the UN SDGs.
evidence_signals: AI project documentation maps outcomes to specific SDGs
Sustainability impact assessments are conducted annually
Environmental footprint (e.g., energy use) of AI models is measured and reported
Stakeholders include sustainability experts in AI governance
Public reports show progress toward SDG targets via AI interventions
evidence_anti_signals: No mention of SDGs or sustainability in AI project plans
AI systems consume excessive energy with no efficiency goals
Environmental or social harm from AI deployment is ignored
controls.remediation_template: If AI systems lack sustainability assessments, integrate SDG-aligned impact evaluations and monitor environmental and social outcomes throughout the life cycle.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Respect, protect, and promote privacy throughout AI life cycle
controls.control_text_full: Privacy, a right essential to the protection of human dignity, human autonomy and human agency, must be respected, protected and promoted throughout the life cycle of AI systems. It is important that data for AI systems be collected, used, shared, archived and deleted in ways that are consistent with international law and in line with the values and principles set forth in this Recommendation, while respecting relevant national, regional and international legal frameworks.
controls.intent_summary: Privacy must be upheld at every stage of AI development and use, with data handling fully compliant with international legal standards and human rights principles.
evidence_signals: Data collection follows purpose limitation and data minimization
Data retention policies specify deletion timelines
Privacy impact assessments are documented and approved
Data subjects can exercise rights (access, correction, deletion)
Encryption and anonymization are applied to sensitive data
evidence_anti_signals: Data is collected without informed consent
Personal data is stored indefinitely without justification
No mechanism for users to delete their data
controls.remediation_template: If privacy is not protected throughout the AI life cycle, implement data governance aligned with international standards and ensure user rights are enforceable.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Establish multi-stakeholder data protection frameworks
controls.control_text_full: Adequate data protection frameworks and governance mechanisms should be established in a multi-stakeholder approach at the national or international level, protected by judicial systems, and ensured throughout the life cycle of AI systems. Data protection frameworks and any related mechanisms should take reference from international data protection principles and standards concerning the collection, use and disclosure of personal data and exercise of their rights by data subjects while ensuring a legitimate aim and a valid legal basis for the processing of personal data, including informed consent.
controls.intent_summary: Data protection for AI must be governed by multi-stakeholder frameworks, legally enforceable, and grounded in international standards with informed consent as a baseline.
evidence_signals: Data protection authority is involved in AI governance
Legal basis for data processing is documented and consent is verifiable
Multi-stakeholder advisory board includes civil society and data subjects
Judicial recourse is available for data rights violations
Framework references GDPR, Convention 108+, or equivalent
evidence_anti_signals: Data processing lacks legal basis or consent
Only private actors control data governance
No judicial or regulatory oversight mechanism
controls.remediation_template: If data protection lacks multi-stakeholder or legal grounding, establish a formal governance framework aligned with international standards and ensure judicial enforceability.
controls.framework_name_en: UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
controls.framework_name_ar: توصية اليونسكو بشأن أخلاقيات الذكاء الاصطناعي

controls.control_title: Conduct privacy impact assessments with ethical considerations
controls.control_text_full: Algorithmic systems require adequate privacy impact assessments, which also include societal and ethical considerations of their use and an innovative use of the privacy by design approach. AI actors need to ensure that they are accountable for the design and implementation of AI systems in such a way as to ensure that personal information is protected throughout the life cycle of the AI system.
controls.intent_summary: Privacy impact assessments for AI must go beyond technical compliance to include societal and ethical impacts, and must embed privacy-by-design principles from the outset.
evidence_signals: Privacy impact assessments include societal risk analysis (e.g., stigma, exclusion)
Privacy-by-design is documented in system architecture diagrams
Ethical review boards evaluate AI’s societal implications
Design documents show data protection integrated into early development
Assessments are updated when AI use cases evolve
evidence_anti_signals: Privacy assessment is a checkbox exercise with no ethical analysis
Privacy features added as afterthoughts, not designed in
No record of societal impact evaluation
